coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Pacquiao, Melvin R.
de Luna, Mark Daniel G.
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Kladsomboon, Sumana
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are optically unique materials for sensing applications that can be prepared from various raw natural materials. In this work, highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from enokitake mushroom via a one-step hydrothermal method in the presence of diluted sulfuric acid. The spherical carbon dots, with an average diameter of 4 nm, exhibited blue photoluminescence under UV illumination and a quantum yield of 11%, increasing to 39% upon passivation with tetraethylenepentamine. We employed both photoluminescence and light absorption properties of carbon dots for Cr6+ and volatile organic compound (VOC) sensing. Their fluorescence emission was selectively quenched by Cr6+ with a limit of detection of 0.73 µM. A fluorescent colorimetric paper-based device was fabricated and demonstrated to determine Cr6+ at concentrations as low as 10 µM. The carbon dots were also shown to have sensitivity and selectivity towards VOCs when integrated into an optical electronic nose. They were then used successfully to determine alcohol contents in aqueous solutions and ethanol concentration in a real spirit sample. They were further tested as a fingerprint detection agent and fluorophore for the manufacture of fluorescent plastics. Our results demonstrated that enokitake mushroom can be used to produce multi-faceted carbon dots with diverse applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Enokitake mushroom
Metal ion sensing
VOC sensing
Electronic nose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the governmental budget through Thammasat University , Thailand in the fiscal year 2017 (Contract No. 6/2560 ). The authors acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Division of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots, (b) survey XPS spectrum of carbon dots, and the high resolution of (c) C1s, and (d) O1s peaks.
(a) TEM image and (b) particle size distribution.

(a) UV-vis absorption and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots measured at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 480 nm. Insets show images of carbon dots under visible light (left) and 365-nm UV light (right).
Effects of (a) reaction time, (b) percent acid, (c) temperature on the quantum yield of carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots (C-Dots) in the presence of various metal ions (100 µM), (b) relative fluorescence intensity of carbon dots, and (c) plot of sum of RGB values and visual images of carbon dot-coated filter paper in the presence of metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots in the presence of Cr6+ at different concentrations, (b) Stern-Volmer equation and plot where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Cr6+, and (c) sum of RGB values and visual images of carbon dots at different Cr6+ concentrations extracted from carbon dot-coated paper.
Schematic diagram of optical electronic nose system.

(a) Gas sensing response of carbon dot film under blue-green light (472 nm), upon exposure to solvent vapors, (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots, and c) UV-vis spectra of carbon dot solutions dissolved in various solvents.
Scheme representing a change of optical properties of carbon dots (CD) in the presence of polar solvents.

Gas sensing responses of carbon dot film under green light for (a) methanol solutions, (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots of methanol solutions, (c) gas sensing responses of carbon dot film for ethanol solutions and spirit sample (35% ethanol v/v), and (d) PCA two-dimensional score plots of ethanol solutions and spirit sample.
(a) Fingerprint images under daylight and UV illumination after 0, 24, and 48 h, and (b) image of PMMA/carbon dot composite under UV illumination.
Detection limits of carbon dots for Cr6+ from previous studies and this work.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Graduate School of Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman, 1101 Quezon City, Philippines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, 1101 Quezon City, Philippines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathum Thani 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Nakhon Pathom 73170, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are optically unique materials for sensing applications that can be prepared from various raw natural materials. In this work, highly fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from enokitake mushroom via a one-step hydrothermal method in the presence of diluted sulfuric acid. The spherical carbon dots, with an average diameter of 4 nm, exhibited blue photoluminescence under UV illumination and a quantum yield of 11%, increasing to 39% upon passivation with tetraethylenepentamine. We employed both photoluminescence and light absorption properties of carbon dots for Cr6+ and volatile organic compound (VOC) sensing. Their fluorescence emission was selectively quenched by Cr6+ with a limit of detection of 0.73 µM. A fluorescent colorimetric paper-based device was fabricated and demonstrated to determine Cr6+ at concentrations as low as 10 µM. The carbon dots were also shown to have sensitivity and selectivity towards VOCs when integrated into an optical electronic nose. They were then used successfully to determine alcohol contents in aqueous solutions and ethanol concentration in a real spirit sample. They were further tested as a fingerprint detection agent and fluorophore for the manufacture of fluorescent plastics. Our results demonstrated that enokitake mushroom can be used to produce multi-faceted carbon dots with diverse applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of carbon dots, reported by Xu et al. in 2004, paved the way for new environmentally friendly carbon-based nanomaterials, now known as carbon quantum dots, carbon nanodots, or carbon dots, with comparable properties to semiconducting quantum dots [1–4]. Carbon dots are fluorescent, spherical carbon nanoparticles normally with particle sizes below 10 nm. Their additionally unique properties include low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, straightforward functionalization, biocompatibility, and high aqueous solubility [5–8]. A range of synthesis approaches have been developed for their production, including combustion [9], ultrasonic treatment [10], arc discharge [1], electrochemical deposition [11], laser ablation [12], and hydrothermal methods [13]. Among these techniques, the hydrothermal method, normally with an addition of acid catalyst, has been applied to the synthesis of carbon dots as it is used with inexpensive apparatus and mild reaction conditions and can produce highly fluorescent carbon dots in high yields. In the presence of the acid catalysts and high temperature and pressure, the precursors undergo hydrolysis, oxidation, and carbonization, resulting in the formation of carbon dots with hydrophilic functional groups on the surface. These functional groups are excellent chelating ligands for metal ions and small molecules. Upon binding between these groups and ions or molecules, the optical properties of carbon dots can be modulated, allowing their use as sensors. Furthermore, due to their nanometer size and high surface area, carbon dots are therefore excellent chemical and bio-probes with high selectivity and sensitivity for a variety of sensing applications [14,15].

Chromium is widely used in a range of industries. It exists in two main forms in aqueous media: trivalent (Cr3+) and hexavalent (Cr6+). Cr3+ is beneficial to living organisms as it is involved in the metabolic processing of protein, fat, and sugar [16]. However, Cr6+ is a potent carcinogen and mutagen even at trace amounts [17]. There is therefore a need for development of selective, sensitive, and practical sensors to trace the Cr6+. There have been very few reports on the fluorescent colorimetric method of Cr6+ detection. Recently, Bu et al. reported the use of phosphate-functionalized carbon dots prepared from ethylenediamine and phytic acid for Cr6+ detection with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 µM [18]. Wang et al. reported the use of chitosan functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and isophorone diisocyanate [19]. However, the preparation of carbon dots reported previously normally involved multiple steps or the addition of special passivating agents or the use of expensive precursors, which can be troublesome from a practical point of view. In the current study, we produced unfunctionalized carbon dots from readily available enokitake mushroom that were selective to Cr6+ with comparable sensitivity to previous reports and used these to prepare a fluorescent colorimetric paper-based sensor. The use of unfunctionalized carbon dots from readily available precursors is advantageous due to the simplicity and convenience as the as-prepared carbon dots can be directly applied for sensing experiments without further treatments. The RGB values were extracted for accurate determination of the Cr6+ concentration with high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition to attractive fluorescent properties, carbon dots possess unique optical absorption, but the use of optical absorption properties of carbon dots for sensing applications is extremely rare. Therefore, in this work, the carbon dots were also employed as a sensing layer to detect volatile organic compounds (VOCs) via an optical electronic nose. The electronic nose is an artificial system that is sensitive to vapors. It is widely used in safety and quality control in food, pharmaceutical, clinical, and chemical industries. [20–22]. VOCs pose serious health and environmental problems, as most are toxic or flammable. Hence, detection of the VOCs using straightforward and practical device is of great interest. We also tested the carbon dots as a fingerprint detection agent and as a fluorophore for the manufacture of fluorescent plastics.

In this work, the carbon dots were prepared from enokitake mushroom as this edible fungus is a readily available and cheap precursor that can be purchased from most supermarkets. The selection of the precursors plays a key role in the physical and chemical properties of the carbon dots. Natural materials are abundant and inexpensive and contain essential elements including C, N, O, S, and P, which provide the carbon dots with unique surface functional groups and properties [23]. A diverse array of raw materials have been used with the hydrothermal method for the carbon dot synthesis, including aloe [24], bagasse [25], garlic [26], grass [13], ginger [27], honey [28], Jinhua bergamot [29], lime [30], orange juice [31], orange peel waste [32], papaya [33], pineapple juice [34], pipe tobacco [35], pomelo peel [36], rose-heart radish [37], sweet potato [38], tamarind [39], lotus root [40], mangosteen pulp [41] etc. However, there have been very few reports on the use of mushroom for carbon dot production. Mushrooms are cultivated in many tropical countries and commercially marketed around the world [42]. Mushrooms are recognized as an alternative source of good quality protein and are rich in vitamins, minerals, and nutrients, indicating various heteroatoms that can yield for highly fluorescent carbon dots. Recently, Wang and coworkers reported the synthesis of shiitake mushroom-derived carbon dots, showing excellent photoluminescence and biomedical and sensing properties [43]. However, to the best of our knowledge, enokitake mushroom (Flammulina velutipes) has not been used for the preparation of carbon dots. The enokitake mushroom has a long, slender white stem, and white cap. It contains bioactive compounds, including polysaccharides, protein-glucan complex, sterols, lectins, peroxidases, laccases, cellulases, and proteases [44]. Its production and consumption ranks fourth among edible mushrooms behind Agaricus bisporus, Lentula edodes, and Pleurotus spp [45–47]. In summary, we developed a synthesis method for preparing highly fluorescent carbon dots from cheap, readily available enokitake mushroom and tested the carbon dots for applications in Cr6+ and volatile organic compound (VOC) sensing, fingerprint imaging, and the fabrication of fluorescent plastics. This was done to investigate the versatility of carbon dots. Fingerprint analysis has been used to identify suspects and solve crimes. Various fluorescent dyes such as ninhydrin, indocyanine green, and fluorescein have been used, but these organic dyes are relatively expensive and toxic. Fluorescent plastics are used in many consumer products, such as toys, paints, and banknotes. Carbon dots can therefore be an alternative non-toxic, cheap fluorophore for various applications.

Fresh enokitake mushrooms and spirit were purchased from a local supermarket. Tetraethylene pentamine and quinine sulfate were purchased from Acros Organics. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (96% v/v) was obtained from Carlo Erba. High grade regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) was obtained from Membrane Filtration Products, Inc. FeSO4, CuBr, NaI, Ni(NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, Fe2(SO4)3, KNO3, PbCl2, CuSO4⋅5H2O, SnCl2⋅H2O, Co(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, LiCl, Al(NO3)2, and TiO2 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of laboratory grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments.

Fresh, cleaned, and crushed enokitake mushroom (20 g) was dispersed in water (100 mL) and 5% v/v H2SO4. The mixture was placed in a 250-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 250 °C for 4 h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was gradually cooled to 25 °C. A homogeneous brown liquid product was obtained, indicating the formation of water-soluble carbon dots. The solution was purified through a 0.22 µm polyethersulphone membrane. The filtrate was subjected to dialysis (MWCO 1000 Da) for 72 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm at 25 °C for 25 min to remove precipitates and agglomerated particles. The resulting yellow solution was freeze-dried to obtain the carbon dots as powder (15% mass yield).

The morphology, uniformity, and microstructure of the carbon dots were analyzed using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The particle diameter was estimated from ImageJ software analysis of the TEM image. The accelerating voltage was 200 kV and the magnification was 100,000×. The functional groups on the carbon dot surface were analyzed using a PharmaSpec Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer, using the frequency range 400–4000 cm−1 at 25 °C. The particle size and zeta potential of the carbon dots were measured using a nanoparticle analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100). The surface elements were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy or XPS (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester U). The UV–vis absorption was determined using a Shimadzu UV-1700 UV–vis spectrophotometer in the absorption wavelength 200–700 nm at 25 °C. The fluorescence emission of the carbon dots was examined using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrometer equipped with excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm and a Xenon lamp. A standard quartz cuvette having a path length of 1.0 cm was used.

The relative quantum yield (quantum yield, Φ) of the fluorescent carbon dots was determined following an established procedure [25]. Quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.54) as reference was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index, η, of 1.33) while the carbon dot samples were dissolved in DI water (η = 1.33). Their fluorescence spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity is the area under the fluorescence curve in the wavelength range 380–700 nm. The data was plotted and the slopes of the sample and the standard were determined. The quantum yield was calculated using Eq. (1),
(1)
Φ
X
=
Φ
R
Grad
X
Grad
R
η
X
2
η
R
2
where Φ is the quantum yield, Grad is the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and R and X denote the reference (standard) and sample, respectively [48].

Multiple metal ions (Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+ and Ti4+) were used in the selectivity experiments. Metal ion solutions (100 µM) were prepared in DI water. A 1.5 mL solution of each metal ion was mixed with a carbon dot solution (3.5 mL, 1 g L−1) and the mixture was allowed to sit at 25 °C for 20 min. The mixture was then transferred to a quartz cuvette, and the fluorescence emission was recorded. Metal ions that showed significant fluorescence quenching were further studied to investigate their detection with carbon dots. Different concentrations of metal ion solutions (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μM) were prepared at the same ratio of metal ions to carbon dot solution. After setting at 25 °C for 20 min, the mixture was transferred to a quartz cuvette, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The measurement of each sample was repeated at least five times.

An optical electronic nose system comprises a light source, sensing materials, and a photo-detector. In this study, light-emitting diodes (LEDs) provided the light source and a commercial photo-detector (ET-TCS230) was used to measure the light intensity. Eight gas sensor arrays were generated, based on the eight colors of the LEDs: infrared, red (638 nm), yellow (587 nm), green (537 nm), blue (457 nm), blue-green (472 nm), violet (399 nm), and white. The light intensity transmitted through a sensing thin film was measured [49]. All measurements were carried out at room temperature. The carbon dot film was prepared on a 2.5 × 2.5 cm glass slide by drop-casting a carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 30 g L−1). The film was then allowed to dry at 60 °C overnight. The gas response of the carbon dot thin film was tested under a dynamic gas flow through a home-made chamber. Nitrogen (99.99%) at a flow rate of approximately 500 mL min−1 into the sensor chamber was used as the reference and carrier gas. A solenoid valve switched the sensor system between this reference gas and the solvent vapors.

A 10 g L−1 solution of carbon dots was prepared, placed on glass slides, and heated in an oven for 10 min. A fingertip was pressed onto the semi-dry carbon dots and the carbon dot covered-fingertip were transferred to a clean transparent surface.

The carbon dots (10 mg) were dissolved in acetone (10 mL), and the solution was mixed thoroughly with 10 g of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). The composite was cast as a film and dried in the oven at 60 °C for 24 h. Images of the fingerprint under UV irradiation (365 nm) at predetermined time intervals and those of the fluorescent plastic were taken using the 13 MP rear camera of a Samsung galaxy J5 (2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, enokitake mushroom was used a precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots using a hydrothermal method. We assumed that, in the presence of sulfuric acid as a dehydrating agent and under high pressure and temperature, the mushroom underwent dehydration/hydrolysis and a carbonization reaction, producing carbon dots [43,50]. The purified carbon dots showed blue fluorescence emission in solution under UV illumination. They were used as Cr6+ and VOC sensing probes and for fingerprint imaging and the fabrication of fluorescent plastics (Fig. 1 ).

The obtained carbon dots were characterized to confirm their structure and optical properties. First, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize their functional groups. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) showed characteristic absorption bands of C-H stretching vibration at 2929 cm−1, OH stretching vibration at 3403 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1,139 cm−1, and CO stretching vibration at 1644 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The presence of these functional groups confers hydrophilicity and aqueous solubility on carbon dots [51]. A further signal at around 624 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration band of SC [52], indicating the presence of sulfur-containing functional groups, possibly originating from the sulfuric acid used as a reagent.

The elemental existence and composition of the carbon dots were investigated using XPS. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) showed the carbon dots to contain carbon and oxygen species at 284 eV (C1s) and 531 eV (O1s), respectively. Small signals of nitrogen and sulfur were found at 399 eV (N1s) and 168 eV (S2p), respectively. The elemental composition of the carbon dots included carbon (70%), oxygen (17%), nitrogen (8%), and sulfur (0.2%). This indicated that the carbon dots had become lightly doped with these heteroatoms from the amino acids and proteins present in the mushroom and the sulfuric acid used as a reagent. The signal at 100 eV represented silicon, which was used as a substrate in XPS sample preparation. The high resolution of the C1s signal (Fig. 2c) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.9, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CO of carboxyl groups, consistent with the FT-IR results. Similarly, the O1s peak (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into peaks at 531.3, 532.4, and 533.7 eV, attributed to CO, COH and COC groups, respectively [33,52–55]. The N2 s peak also consists of pyridinic and pyrrolinic nitrogen at 400.1 and 401.8 eV whereas the S2p peak consists of SC and SO peaks at 167.7 and 169.1 eV, respectively.

The particle size and morphology of the carbon dots were characterized using TEM. The TEM image of the carbon dots (Fig. 3 a) showed them to be spherical and well dispersed. From the histogram of the size distribution (Fig. 3b), the diameters of the spherical carbon dots were in the range 1–9 nm with an average of 4 nm. This is consistent with a previous report on blue-fluorescent carbon dots [26]. The high-resolution TEM image shows a lattice spacing of about 0.3 nm, close to that of graphite. This confirms the presence of graphitic core within the carbon dots. The broad dispersal was in agreement with the highly negative zeta potential (−69 mV) at the surface of the carbon dots. The highly negative zeta potential possibly arises because enokitake is rich in amino acids, due to the high protein content, consistent with the high nitrogen content identified using XPS [56].

The optical properties of the carbon dots were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. A strong optical absorption was found in the UV region (Fig. 4 a). The absorption spectrum had a shoulder at around 250–285 nm, corresponding to π–π∗ transition of the conjugated CC bonds and aromatic sp2 domains [57,58]. The long absorption tail up to 600 nm is due to the low energy transitions within the surface states, created by the surface functional groups of carbon dots [59]. The fluorescence emission spectra showed a highest emission peak at 470 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 4b). The inset images show carbon dots in aqueous solution under visible light (left) and UV illumination at 365 nm (right). The well-dispersed aqueous carbon dot solution exhibited bright blue emission under UV light. As in previous studies, the prepared carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent emission. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 480 nm, the emission maxima changed from the blue to yellow region (360–550 nm). The strongest fluorescence emission was found at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence emission of carbon dots is due to the radiative recombination between electrons and holes generated and trapped at the surface sites on the carbon dots. Various surface functional groups of carbon dots provide various emissive states that lead to excitation-dependent emission [39,60,61]. Furthermore, distribution of particle size also contributes to this excitation-dependent phenomenon [61,62]. This was confirmed by our FTIR, XPS, and TEM results.

To maximize the quantum yield of carbon dots, the effects of reaction time, temperature, and acid concentration of the carbon dots were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, we found the quantum yields of 11%, 7%, and 1% at reaction times of 4, 6, and 8 h, respectively, using 5% v/v H2SO4 at 250 °C [35,38,63]. The effect of acid concentration on the quantum yield (Fig. 5b) was investigated using a reaction time of 4 h and temperature of 250 °C. The acid concentrations of 1%, 3% and 5% gave a quantum yield of 4%, 8% and 11%, respectively. This suggested that the acid produced carbon dots with favorable emission states. Lastly, the effect of temperature on the quantum yield was studied at 180, 200, and 250 °C, producing quantum yields of 4%, 6%, and 11%, respectively under optimized conditions of 4 h and an acid concentration of 5% v/v (Fig. 5c). Higher temperatures also produced brighter carbon dots [26]. Overall, a shorter reaction time, higher temperature, and higher acid concentration resulted in higher quantum yields. The highest quantum yield (11%) was from carbon dots prepared using 5% v/v H2SO4, a reaction time of 4 h, and a temperature of 250 °C. The quantum yield was further enhanced by using nitrogen-rich tetraethylenepentamine (TEPE) as a surface passivation agent. The passivation of the carbon dots produced a quantum yield of 39%. This result is consistent with previous reports that nitrogen-doped carbon dots typically possess high quantum yields [23,64,65]. This result demonstrates that our carbon dots can be modified to increase the quantum yields to be applicable for applications that require highly photoluminescent materials.

Carbon dots are regarded as a unique fluorescent chemical and bio-sensing probe [66]. Their hydrophilic functional groups are excellent chelating ligands for metal ions, making them excellent metal ion sensors. Upon adsorption of metal ions, the fluorescence emission of carbon dots is modulated. In this study, the carbon dots were used for trace analysis of metal ions. Pollution by metal ions has become a critical issue, posing severe risks to organisms and the environment, and impacting human health [67]. The selectivity of carbon dots was determined using a range of heavy metal ions: Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Ti2+ (Fig. 6 a and b). The carbon dot and metal ions were mixed in solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. We found that Cr6+ ions quenched and reduced the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots by 85%, while other ions produced only a small change. The mechanism underlying the significant and selective fluorescence quenching of Cr6+ ions is due to the inner filter effect [18,19]. The inner filter effect occurs when there is an overlapping between optical absorption of quencher and emission of fluorophore. We found that optical absorption of Cr6+ overlapped with the emission spectrum of carbon dots at around 400–500 nm (Fig. S1), confirming that the inner filter effect is responsible for the fluorescent quenching of carbon dots by Cr6+.

We tested the selectivity of dots to Cr6+ when loaded onto Whatman No.1 cellulose-based filter paper. The paper was immersed in a 0.05 g L−1 carbon dot solution. After 5 min, the paper was removed and dried at room temperature. The metal ion test solutions (100 μM) were then individually dropped onto the coated filter paper, which was placed under a UV lamp and an image was taken and RGB values were extracted. As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the fluorescence emission of the coated paper became darker after exposure to Cr6+, and was visible to the naked eye. This darkening was less pronounced after exposure to the other metal ions. Consistently, the sum of RGB value of carbon dots with Cr6+ was obviously the lowest, compared with other metal ions. This was attributed to quenching of the fluorescence emission by the Cr6+, and demonstrated turn-off fluorescence upon addition of Cr6+ ions to the carbon dot-coated filter paper. This portable fluorescent colorimetric paper-based sensor has potential applications in Cr6+ sensing.

We next tested the carbon dot-coated filter paper with Cr6+ ions at different concentrations. Fig. 7c shows images taken under UV illumination. Naked eye inspection confirmed that the image color became distinguishable from that of the control sample (without Cr6+) at concentrations greater than 10 µM. The sum of RGB values of the images were extracted for more accurate color determination, and were found to diverge significantly from those of the control sample at 10 μM. The carbon dot-coated filter paper was therefore able to effectively sense Cr6+ at concentrations as low as 10 μM, suggesting that the carbon dots developed in this work would make excellent Cr6+ sensors.

The effects of solvents on the light absorption of carbon dots was investigated. Instead of preparing thin film of carbon dots, we dissolved carbon dots in the solvents, filtered the insoluble residues, and measured the absorbance of the solutions because a thin film produced a small change of absorbance upon exposure to solvents and that would be difficult to differentiate the effects of solvent using UV–vis spectroscopy. We found that the absorbance of carbon dots in different solvents can be ranked as follows: water > methanol > dichloromethane > hexane (Fig. 9c). In other words, the absorbance increases as the polarity increases. This is because carbon dots are hydrophilic and is more soluble in more polar solvents. This result agrees well with the polarity trend obtained from optical electronic nose result. This suggests that the polar-polar interaction led to a change in the optical properties of carbon dots, which can be observed using optical electronic nose. The solvent-dependent optical properties of carbon dots have been previously observed in various carbon dot-based materials. For example, Wang et al. reported that the Mn(II)-doped carbon dots showed stronger light absorption in polar solvents than in nonpolar ones [69]. Similarly, Zheng et al. reported that the carbon dots synthesized from (Z)-4-(2-cyano-2-(4′-(diphenylamino)-[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-vinyl)benzonitrile had higher optical absorption in polar solvents [70]. The change of optical properties of carbon dot upon exposure to VOCs could be due the change of polarization, solvent relaxation, and energy transfer between solvent molecules and carbon dots [59] (Fig. 10 ). This can be further confirmed by transient spectroscopic and computational studies, which are underway.

Since our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose is most sensitive to methanol and ethanol vapors, we further investigated the sensitivity of our system towards different methanol and ethanol solutions. Aqueous alcohol solutions (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) were prepared and used as vapor sources. The magnitudes of the sensor responses confirmed that the carbon dot film could sense both methanol and ethanol vapors at those concentrations (Fig. 11 a and c). These results are consistent with the experimental results reported above, as the sensor response decreased rapidly when the alcohol vapor was flowed to the film. The PCA score plots were analyzed to show the data points of different methanol and ethanol concentrations. We found that PCA data points of methanol and ethanol solutions were well separated with at least 99.9% total variance (Fig. 11b and d). This indicates that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose can be used to specify alcohol concentrations. We further tested the ability of the carbon dots to analyze the ethanol content of a real spirit sample with 35% v/v ethanol content, specified on the bottle label. The scattered data points of the spirit sample were found to overlap well with those of the 25% and 50% standard ethanol solutions, indicating the acceptable accuracy of our sensor system. Therefore, the carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose developed in this work has potential applications in vapor sensing for the chemical, food, and beverage industries. It is noteworthy to point out that our electronic nose can also sense water vapor. This shows the potentials to be also used as humidity sensor. At the moment, although the sensitivity of carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose presented in this work is about one order of magnitude lower than those of other alcohol vapor sensors in previous reports, such as metal-based materials [71], conductive polymers [72], carbon nanotubes [73], and graphene-based materials [74], the carbon dots are much simpler to be prepared, compared to those aforementioned materials. Furthermore, we demonstrated the potentials of using carbon dots in a much wider range of applications as this is the first time that the optical absorption properties of carbon dots could be utilized for vapor sensing. Further improvements can be performed, for example, by surface passivation of carbon dots and the use of highly porous materials as composites, in order to increase the sensitivity.

The carbon dots were next tested as an imaging probe for fingerprint detection and in fluorescent plastics in order to demonstrate that the carbon dots are also solid-state luminescent materials. We first used the carbon dots to image the human fingerprint. Fig. 12 a shows images of the human fingerprint under daylight and UV light. The thumbprint was observable after 48 h, demonstrating the photostability of the carbon dots. The non-toxic, easy-to-use, and water-soluble carbon dots developed in this work have potential uses in fingerprint detection and imaging, which will be particularly useful for crime scene investigation.

The carbon dots have further potential applications as fluorescent ink for security papers and banknotes [75,76]. In this study, the carbon dots were used for the preparation of fluorescent plastics. PMMA has excellent transparency, and has been widely used for the fabrication of optical fibers, optical disks, and lenses [77]. An image was captured of PMMA/carbon dot-based plastic under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 12b). The carbon dots exhibited blue and uniform fluorescence, showing good dispersion in the PMMA. It also suggested excellent compatibility between the hydrophobic PMMA and hydrophilic carbon dots. This would allow highly luminescent carbon dots to be incorporated into polymer matrices, opening a wide range of applications, including flat panel displays and anti-counterfeiting labeling [78,79].

This work reports a facile synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from enokitake mushrooms via a one-step, environmentally-friendly hydrothermal process. The addition of nitrogen-rich tetraethylenepentamine improved the quantum yield to 39%. While in most studies, carbon dots were employed as fluorescent probes, we applied both their fluorescence and light absorption properties for Cr6+ and VOC sensing. The unmodified carbon dots showed blue photoluminescence at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and were found to be effective for sensing and detection of Cr6+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity. They remained effective when coated onto a paper sensor. They also acted as a sensing material for the detection of methanol and ethanol vapors via optical electronic nose and as a solid-state luminescent material for the detection of fingerprints and preparation of fluorescent plastics. The versatile carbon dots reported in this work will be applicable for several sensing and optical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the governmental budget through Thammasat University, Thailand in the fiscal year 2017 (Contract No. 6/2560). The authors acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Division of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.04.199.
